docs.title: The Infinite Black 2 Wiki
docs.text:
Welcome to the community-run wiki for The Infinite Black 2, the cross-platform space MMO.

Under Construction

This wiki is currently being built by the community. Feel free to contribute on GitHub! Reach out to Goot in the game for any questions or suggestions!

docs.title: Quick Links
docs.text:
• Ships - Detailed stats for all ship classes.
  • Weapons - Damage types and weapon tiers.
  • Corporations - How to manage your corp.
  • Maps - Navigating the sectors.

docs.title: Game Info
docs.text:
• Developer: Spellbook
  • Genre: Space MMO
  • Status: Live

docs.title: Contributing
docs.text:
Welcome! This section explains how to contribute to the wiki, how the folder structure works, and where to find examples and templates you can copy.

The goal is to make contributing easy, predictable, and low-risk — even if you’re new to MkDocs, Obsidian, or docs-as-code workflows.

docs.title: How this folder is structured
docs.text:
The Contributing/ folder is a self-contained guide that mirrors how the rest of the wiki is organized.

Contributing/
   ├─ index.md ← You are here (section overview)
   ├─ FAQ.md ← Common contributor questions
   ├─ Changelog.md ← Changes to contribution rules/templates
   ├─ Draft Notes.md ← (hidden from navigation)
   ├─ Getting Started/
   │  ├─ index.md ← Step-by-step guides & explanations
   │  └─ Example.md ← Concrete examples you can copy
   └─ .pages ← Navigation rules (infrastructure)

A few important notes:

  • Every navigable folder has an index.md that acts as a landing page
  • Subfolders represent logical sections, not just file storage
  • Some files may exist but be hidden from navigation
  • Navigation order is controlled locally via a .pages file

You don’t need to edit .pages unless you are changing navigation behavior.

docs.title: Adding a new page to this folder
docs.text:
1. Create a new Markdown file in Contributing/
  2. Give it a clear, descriptive name
  3. If it’s an important page:
  4. Add it explicitly to Contributing/.pages
  5. If it’s minor or supplemental:
  6. Let it be picked up automatically via ...

Example:

Contributing/Style Guide.md

docs.title: Adding a new subfolder (section)
docs.text:
When adding a new folder:

  1. Create the folder
  2. Add an index.md inside it (required)
  3. (Optional but recommended) Add a .pages file to control ordering
  4. Update the parent folder’s .pages if this is a major section

Example:

Contributing/New Section/
   ├─ index.md
   ├─ Examples.md
   └─ .pages

docs.title: How navigation works
docs.text:
Navigation is filesystem-driven, not centralized.

  • The sidebar is generated automatically from folders and files
  • Ordering, visibility, and titles are controlled by .pages files
  • Some content may exist only for reference and not appear in navigation

If something doesn’t show up in the sidebar: - Check the nearest .pages file - Look for hide: rules - Look for explicit nav: lists

docs.title: Where to find examples and templates
docs.text:
Start here: Getting Started

That section contains: - Step-by-step examples - Sample folder layouts - Pages you can safely copy and adapt - Explanations of common patterns used across the wiki

If you’re unsure how to structure something, there’s probably an example there already.

docs.title: General contribution philosophy
docs.text:
• Navigation is curated — not everything needs to be in the sidebar
  • Search is automatic — content is discoverable even if hidden
  • Folders represent concepts, not just storage
  • Consistency beats cleverness

If you’re ever unsure, copy an existing pattern and adjust it slightly.

If something here is unclear, outdated, or missing — that’s a contribution opportunity in itself 🙂

docs.title: Draft Notes
docs.text: This file is hidden, so it won't show up in the deployed nav bar, but it is still searchable. Check Contributing/.pages for an example of how to hide pages from the nav.

docs.title: FAQ
docs.text: Commonly asked questions about folder structure should go here.

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text:
This guide walks you through the three most common contribution tasks step by step:

  1. Modifying text on an existing page
  2. Adding a new page
  3. Adding a new folder (section)

You do not need to understand the entire system to contribute safely.

docs.title: Modifying text on an existing page
docs.text: This is the easiest and safest way to contribute.

docs.title: Step 1: Find the page
docs.text:
• Use search in Obsidian, or
  • Navigate through folders in the file tree

Open the .md file you want to edit.

docs.title: Step 2: Edit the content
docs.text:
• Change text directly
  • Add or remove sections
  • Fix typos, clarify wording, or expand explanations

Use normal Markdown:

## Section heading

Some text here.

You may also use Markdown Links when linking to other pages:

[[Contributing/FAQ|Frequently Asked Questions]]
[[Contributing/index|Contributing]]

docs.title: Step 3: Save and preview
docs.text:
• Save the file
  • Preview in Obsidian if you want
  • Commit your change when ready

That's it. No navigation changes required.

docs.title: Adding a new page
docs.text: Add a new page when you're introducing new content within an existing section.

docs.title: Step 1: Choose the correct folder
docs.text:
Pick the folder that best matches the topic.

Example:

Gameplay/

If you're unsure, check for similar pages nearby and follow that pattern.

docs.title: Step 2: Create the file
docs.text:
Create a new Markdown file with a clear, descriptive name:

Gameplay/Status Effects.md

Avoid vague names like: - Notes.md - Stuff.md - New Page.md

docs.title: Step 3: Add basic structure
docs.text:
Start with a title and a short introduction:

# Status Effects

This page describes status effects that can be applied to entities in the game.

Add sections as needed.

docs.title: Step 4: Decide if it belongs in navigation
docs.text:
• Important page?
    • Add it explicitly to the folder's .pages file
  • Minor/supporting page?
    • Let it be included automatically via ...

If you don't know, default to automatic.

docs.title: Adding a new folder (section)
docs.text: Add a new folder when you're introducing a new conceptual area, not just a single page.

docs.title: Step 1: Create the folder
docs.text:
Example:

World/Factions/

docs.title: Step 2: Create an index page (required)
docs.text:
Every navigable folder must have an index.md.

World/Factions/index.md

Minimum recommended content:

# Factions

This section documents the major factions that exist in the game world.

The index.md acts as the landing page for the section.

docs.title: Step 3: (Recommended) Add a .pages file
docs.text:
Create:

World/Factions/.pages

Basic template:

nav:
  - index.md
  - ...

This ensures: - The section is clickable - The index page appears first - Future pages are included automatically

docs.title: Step 4: Add pages inside the folder
docs.text:
Example:

World/Factions/
├─ index.md
├─ The Syndicate.md
├─ Free Traders.md
└─ .pages

Use wikilinks between related pages where appropriate.

docs.title: How to know if you're doing it right
docs.text:
You're probably on the right track if:

  • You copied an existing pattern
  • Your folder has an index.md
  • You didn't fight the navigation system
  • Your change is easy to understand in isolation

Consistency matters more than perfection.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
For concrete examples you can copy, see:

[[Getting Started/Examples/index|Examples]]

If something here feels unclear or awkward, let us know!

docs.title: Markdown Links
docs.text: This guide explains how to link pages correctly in this wiki, with clear rules for when to use relative vs absolute links. Following these conventions keeps links predictable, portable, and compatible with both MkDocs and Obsidian.

docs.title: The Golden Rule
docs.text:
Links should point to source files, not rendered URLs.

Always link to the .md file path inside docs/, never to a slug like /hello-world.

docs.title: Link Types at a Glance
docs.text: Situation Use Example Same folder Relative page.md Child folder Relative Folder/index.md Parent folder Relative ../index.md Distant section Absolute /Section/Page.md Templates / global refs Absolute /Contributing/index.md

docs.title: When to use them
docs.text:
Use relative links when linking to pages that are:

  • In the same folder
  • In a child or parent folder
  • Part of the same section that might be moved together

docs.title: Why
docs.text:
• Mirror the file system
  • Survive navigation changes
  • Work in both MkDocs and Obsidian
  • Easy to reason about

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
Assume this file:

docs/World/hello.md

Same folder

[Maps](maps.md)

Child folder

[Planets](Planets/index.md)

Parent folder

[World overview](../index.md)

Sibling folder

[Ships](../Ships/index.md)

💡 Tip: If you could reach it with cd in a terminal, you can link to it relatively.

docs.title: When to use them
docs.text:
Use absolute links when:

  • Linking across distant sections
  • Referring to canonical pages (FAQ, Contributing, Index pages)
  • Writing templates or examples meant to be copied

docs.title: Format
docs.text:
Absolute links always start at the docs/ root:

/Section/Page.md

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
[Contributing guide](/Contributing/index.md)
[FAQ](/Contributing/FAQ.md)
[Ship classes](/Ships/index.md)

docs.title: Don’t link to rendered URLs
docs.text:
/hello-world

Rendered slugs depend on titles and theme behavior and may change.

docs.title: Recommended Style Rules (Summary)
docs.text:
• Prefer relative links within a section
  • Use absolute links for site-wide references
  • Always link to the .md file, not a rendered URL
  • Do not rely on titles or slugs for navigation

docs.title: One-Sentence Mantra
docs.text: If you can trace the file path with your eyes, use a relative link. If you want a global reference, use an absolute one.

docs.title: External Links (Off‑Wiki References)
docs.text: Markdown links can also point to external websites (outside the wiki). While these are sometimes necessary, they should be used sparingly and intentionally.

docs.title: When External Links Are Appropriate
docs.text:
Use an external link when:

  • The information is authoritative and primary (e.g., official developer posts, patch notes, or APIs).
  • The content is too large or out of scope to reasonably mirror on the wiki.
  • The link provides verification, not opinion (e.g., source citations).

Example:

For official patch notes, see the [developer announcement](https://example.com/patch-notes).

docs.title: Why External Links Should Be Minimized
docs.text:
Excessive external linking can introduce problems:

  • Bias: Linking selectively to certain creators, forums, or communities can imply endorsement or favoritism.
  • Link rot: External pages can change, move, or disappear over time.
  • Fragmented knowledge: Readers are pushed off‑site instead of finding answers within the wiki.

As a rule:

If the information can live on the wiki, it should live on the wiki.

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
• Prefer internal pages over external links whenever possible.
  • If an external link is needed, ensure it is:
    • Neutral and factual
    • Clearly labeled (what the reader should expect)
    • Not required to understand the core content
  • Avoid linking to:
    • Opinion pieces
    • Monetized content (ads, referral links)
    • Single‑person interpretations when a neutral summary can be written instead

docs.title: Recommended Pattern
docs.text:
Write the content locally, then optionally add a source:

This mechanic was introduced in version 1.4 to rebalance fleet combat. 

*Source: [Official patch notes](https://example.com/patch-notes)*

This keeps the wiki self‑contained, neutral, and durable, while still allowing readers to verify information if they choose to.

docs.title: Using Markdown
docs.text: This page provides canonical examples for writing and formatting content on the The Infinite Black 2 Wiki. All contributors should follow these conventions to keep the wiki clean, consistent, and professional.

docs.title: Headings
docs.text:
Use headings to structure pages logically. Do not skip levels.

# Page Title (H1 – only one per page)

## Major Section (H2)

### Subsection (H3)

#### Minor Details (H4)

Rules - Each page must have exactly one H1 at the top. - Use sentence case for headings. - Avoid emojis in headings (allowed in body text if appropriate).

Paragraphs & Line Breaks

Write in clear, concise paragraphs.

This is a paragraph. It should explain a single idea clearly.

This is a new paragraph. Avoid walls of text.

Do not manually add line breaks inside paragraphs.

docs.title: Emphasis
docs.text:
*Italic*  
**Bold**  
***Bold Italic***

Guidelines - Use bold for important terms or first mentions. - Use italic sparingly (titles, emphasis, flavor).

docs.title: Unordered Lists
docs.text:
- Fast 
- Durable
- Expensive

docs.title: Ordered Lists
docs.text:
1. Warp to target 
2. Engage shields 
3. Deploy drones

docs.title: Nested Lists
docs.text:
- Weapons
  - Lasers
  - Missiles
- Defenses  
  - Shields  
  - Armor

docs.title: Links
docs.text:
We use standard markdown links, because the tech lead couldn't figure out how to configure wikilinks plugin with mkdocs properly. If you'd like to attempt it, contact the owners of this wiki.

See Markdown Links for examples of how to link pages.

docs.title: Naming Conventions (Pages)
docs.text: Consistency matters more than perfection.

docs.title: Page Titles
docs.text:
• Use Title Case
  • Be descriptive, not vague

Good - Carrier - Ship Classes - Harvest Drones - Faction: The Pirates

Avoid - car - Ships2 - Stuff - Guide_final_v3

docs.title: Inline Code
docs.text:
Use for commands, keys, or filenames.

Press `F` to interact. Edit the `mkdocs.yml` file.

docs.title: Code Blocks
docs.text:
site_name: The Infinite Black 2 Wiki 
theme:   
  name: material

docs.title: Admonitions (Info Boxes)
docs.text:
Material for MkDocs supports admonitions.

!!! info
    This ship excels at hit-and-run tactics.

!!! warning
    This information may change in future patches.

!!! danger
    Using multiple accounts may result in a ban.

docs.title: Collapsible Sections (Spoilers)
docs.text:
Use for spoilers or optional deep dives.

??? spoiler "Story Spoilers"
    The player captain is a mass-produced clone with no moral compass.

docs.title: Tone & Style
docs.text:
• Write in neutral, informative language
  • Avoid speculation unless clearly labeled
  • No first-person opinions (I think, In my experience)
  • Assume the reader is intelligent but new

Good

This weapon is commonly used in PvP engagements due to its high burst damage.

Avoid

I think this weapon is really good and I always use it.

docs.title: Final Notes
docs.text:
• When in doubt, copy an existing good page
  • Consistency beats cleverness
  • Ask before inventing new conventions

If something isn’t covered here, bring it up with the project lead.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text: List examples here!

docs.title: Corporations
docs.text: Placeholder text

docs.title: Currency List
docs.text:
There are four primary currencies used in The Infinite Black 2.

  • Credits - The standard tradable currency. Used for trading, raffles, and converting to Tech Points.

  • Skulls - The high-value PvP currency. Tradable and used for ship crafting and elite ranks.

  • Tech Points - Bound progression currency.

  • Star Shards - Premium currency.

Tradability

Only Credits and Skulls are directly tradable between players. Tech Points and Star Shards are bound to your account.

Market Watch: Current Trade Ratios

The exchange rate between Credits and Skulls fluctuates based on player activity.

* 1 Skull =~ 700 - 800 Credits

Note: Prices are subject to change based on the current Auction House economy.

docs.title: Credits
docs.text: Credits are the primary standard currency in The Infinite Black 2. They are the lifeblood of the player economy and are fully tradable.

docs.title: How to Obtain
docs.text:
Credits are primarily generated through commerce and recycling:

  • Auction House: Selling items, resources, or equipment to other players.
  • Scrapping Junk: Breaking down "Junk" items found during gameplay.
  • Scrapping Resources: Scrapping piles of Crafted Resources.

docs.title: Uses
docs.text:
Credits have three main functions:

  1. The Auction House: Purchasing items, ships, and gear listed by other players.
  2. Raffles: Buying tickets for Unique Ship raffles.
  3. Tech Point Conversion: Credits can be mass-converted into Tech Points to fuel progression.

Conversion Rate

1,000 Credits $\rightarrow$ 1 Tech Point

docs.title: Skulls
docs.text: Skulls are the elite currency of The Infinite Black 2. They are a mark of combat proficiency and are fully tradable, making them highly valuable on the market.

docs.title: How to Obtain
docs.text:
Skulls are exclusively earned through Player-vs-Player (PvP) activities:

  • Open World PvP: Destroying enemy player ships in non-safe sectors.
  • Structure Warfare: Destroying enemy player-owned structures (POS).
  • The Arena: Earned as a reward for competing in Arena matches.

docs.title: Market Value
docs.text:
Because Skulls are hard to farm but necessary for high-end crafting, they command a high price.

  • Current Value: ~700 - 800 Credits per Skull.

docs.title: Uses
docs.text:
While they can be traded for wealth, Skulls are physically consumed as a material for high-tier manufacturing:

  • Ship Crafting: Required to craft certain ships. insert ship crafting cost table link here
  • Elite Ranks: Required to increase the elite rank of structures and ships. insert elite rank cost table link here

docs.title: Star Shards
docs.text: Star Shards are the premium cosmetic currency in The Infinite Black 2. They are dedicated exclusively to visual customization and do not affect gameplay stats.

docs.title: How to Obtain
docs.text: Star Shards can be acquired through high-level PvE challenges, currency exchange, or direct purchase.

docs.title: 1. Currency Exchange (TIB Market)
docs.text:
You can convert Tech Points into Star Shards via the TIB Market.

Exchange Rate

1,000 Tech Points $\rightarrow$ 2,000 Star Shards (Once per Day)

docs.title: 2. Gameplay Rewards
docs.text: • Outposts: Earned by completing Outpost Mission Chains and defeating the final boss at the end of the chain.

docs.title: 3. Direct Purchase
docs.text: • Cash Shop: Purchasable directly for real money ($).

docs.title: Uses
docs.text:
Currently, Star Shards serve a single purpose:

  • Cosmetics: Purchasing visual upgrades from the TIB Market (e.g., Ship Skins, Engine Trails, Avatars).

docs.title: Tech Points
docs.text:
Tech Points (TP) are the primary utility and progression currency. They are the most versatile currency in the game, used for everything from item upgrades to guild management.

Tradability: Tech Points are Bound and cannot be traded between players.

docs.title: How to Obtain
docs.text:
• Conversion: Purchased using Credits (Standard rate: 1,000 Credits = 1 TP).
  • Gameplay: Rewards for completing Outpost Missions, Missions and unlocking Achievements.
  • Store: Purchasable directly via the Cash Shop ($).

New Player Strategy

Don't spend Tech Points augmenting item rarities early on. You will earn a large amount of them from early game achievements which will slow down as you play longer. It is best practice to save them up and use them on an item that you use long term. Your in-game corporation can help you decide on the best items.

docs.title: Uses: Equipment & Items
docs.text:
Tech Points are the fuel for the item upgrade system:

  • Ranking: Used to Rank items Up or Down.
  • Augmenting: Used to upgrade the Rarity of an item (e.g., Rare $\rightarrow$ Ultra-Rare).

docs.title: Uses: Player Convenience
docs.text:
• Expansion: Purchasing additional slots for your Item Bank and Ship Garage.
  • Travel: Used to Quick Jump to specific locations instantly.
  • Research: Buying "Research Time" for technologies in the player captain research menu.

docs.title: Uses: Corporations
docs.text:
Tech Points are vital for Corporation growth and logistics:

  • Structure Management: Unlocking new corporation Corporation Defense Structures and paying to deploy them into sectors.
  • Expansion: Buying additional Member Slots to recruit more players.
  • Storage: Purchasing extra Corp Bank slots for shared storage.

docs.title: Structures Overview
docs.text: Structures form the backbone of territory control and economy in The Infinite Black 2.

docs.title: Corporation Limits
docs.text:
Every Corporation starts with a base set of structures and can unlock more via progression.

Structure Type Starting Limit Max Unlock Limit Notes Garrisons 1 4 Defensive structures. Outposts 1 8 PvE Mission hubs. Shipyards 1 4 1 variant for each Faction. Planets 1 1 Only 1 Planet allowed per Corp.

docs.title: NPC Factions & Behavior
docs.text: The universe is populated by five major factions: Humans, Wyrd, Hets, Precursors, and Pirates. Their structures behave differently depending on their alignment.

docs.title: Human (Friendly)
docs.text:
• Behavior: Non-hostile. They allow players to deposit items.
  • Roaming: Human Outposts and Shipyards randomly roam the maps.
  • Quick Jump:
    • Garrisons: :white_check_mark: Allowed (if discovered).
    • Outposts/Shipyards: :x: Cannot Quick Jump to these.

docs.title: Enemies (Wyrd, Hets, Precursors, Pirates)
docs.text:
• Behavior: Hostile.
  • Spawning: Structures randomly spawn (and respawn) in PvP Maps and Security 9 PvE Maps controlled by that faction.

docs.title: Deployment
docs.text:
All Corporation structures follow strict rules for placement and movement.

  • View Deployment Rules - Costs, timers, and restriction zones.

docs.title: Structure Deployment
docs.text: Deploying Corporation structures is a strategic action that allows you to claim territory and set up forward operating bases.

docs.title: Deployment Costs & Mechanics
docs.text:
• Cost: 15 Tech Points per deployment.
  • Duration: Structures are permanent until recalled or destroyed (see PvE Exceptions below).
  • Movement: Structures are static once deployed. To move them, they must be recalled and re-deployed.

docs.title: Recall Process
docs.text:
Recalling a structure returns it to the Corporation inventory. Recalls are immediately stopped if a structure is combat flagged in an engagement.

  • Timer: Recalling takes exactly 3 minutes.
    • Note: This timer may fluctuate slightly (by a few seconds) depending on server health/tick rate.

docs.title: Restrictions & Blocking
docs.text: You cannot deploy a structure just anywhere. The following conditions must be met:

docs.title: 1. Sector Exclusivity
docs.text: Only one structure of each type can exist per sector. (e.g., You cannot place a Garrison in a sector that already has a Garrison).

docs.title: 2. Proximity Blocking
docs.text:
Deployment is blocked if Enemy Entities are present in the current sector or any connected sector (1-sector radius).

  • Blocking Entities: Enemy Structures (Player or NPC) and Enemy Player Ships.
  • Non-Blocking Entities: NPC Ships.

docs.title: 3. Combat Flag
docs.text: You cannot deploy structures while your active ship has a Combat Flag. * Workaround: You may select a different, unflagged ship to initiate the deployment.

docs.title: PvE Map Specifics
docs.text:
In PvE maps, to prevent clutter and stagnation, specific limits apply:

  • Map Capacity: There is a limit of 7 Player-Owned Structures per map.
  • Auto-Recall: Structures in PvE maps will automatically recall after 15 Days.

docs.title: Garrisons
docs.text: Garrisons are the primary defensive structures for Corporations, used to secure territory and project power.

docs.title: Human Garrisons (PvE)
docs.text:
In PvE maps, the Human faction maintains a network of Garrisons to aid travel.

  • Location: Always located alongside Jump Gates.
  • Quick Jump: Players can Quick Jump to these Garrisons if they have previously discovered them.

docs.title: Elite Ranks
docs.text:
Higher-tier Garrisons require significant investment but offer vastly improved durability and firepower.

  • Crafting Material: Skulls are required to craft Elite versions of Garrisons.

docs.title: Deployment & Ownership
docs.text:
• Owner: Corporation (Player or NPC).
  • Cost: 15 TP.
  • Restrictions: Subject to Deployment Rules.

docs.title: Jump Gates
docs.text: Jump Gates are static, neutral travel infrastructure found throughout the universe.

docs.title: Functionality
docs.text:
• Ownership: Jump Gates are not owned by any entity (Player or NPC).
  • Behavior: They exist as permanent fixed points connecting specific maps.
  • Combat: Jump Gates cannot be attacked or destroyed.

docs.title: Outposts
docs.text: Outposts are PvE-focused installations that provide content and rewards for players.

docs.title: Human Outposts
docs.text:
• Roaming: Human Outposts randomly roam maps.
  • Travel: You cannot Quick Jump to Human Outposts.
  • Services: They are non-hostile and accept resource deposits.

docs.title: Planets
docs.text: Planets are the economic and social hubs of The Infinite Black 2.

docs.title: Corporation Planets
docs.text:
• Limit: A Corporation may only own 1 Planet.
  • Function: Serves as the central economic hub for the Corporation.

docs.title: Unique Faction Homeworlds
docs.text:
Specific NPC factions have permanent Homeworlds that always respawn at the same coordinates (7 / 7) in their respective Security 9 maps.

Planet Name Faction Map Coordinates Notes Earth Human Sol 7 / 7 The starting point for all new players. Tortuga Pirates Dorado 7 / 7 Eye'lr'tas'ka Hets Vega 7 / 7 The Life Seed Wyrd Rigel 7 / 7 Arc Prime Precursors Orion 7 / 7

docs.title: Deployment
docs.text:
• Cost: 15 TP.
  • Rules: See Deployment Guide.

docs.title: Shipyards
docs.text: Shipyards are Corporation-owned industrial facilities dedicated to the construction and storage of vessels.

docs.title: Capacity & Variants
docs.text:
Corporations can unlock up to 4 Shipyards in total.

  • Faction Variants: There is one Shipyard variant for each of the major factions.
  • Function: Used for crafting faction-specific hulls and storing fleet vessels.

docs.title: Deployment
docs.text:
• Cost: 15 TP.
  • Rules: See Deployment Guide.

docs.title: Test
docs.text: But it appears to be gone!

docs.title: Activities
docs.text: There are various activities available to captains within The Infinite Black.

docs.title: Topics
docs.text:
• PVE – Placeholder value.
  • PVP – Placeholder value.
  • Invasions – Placeholder value.
  • Arena – Placeholder value.

docs.title: Player vs Environment (PVE)
docs.text: PVE Text Placeholder

docs.title: Invasions
docs.text: Invasion placeholder text

docs.title: Player vs Player (PVP)
docs.text: PVP Placeholder Text

docs.title: Arena
docs.text: Arena placeholder text

docs.title: The World
docs.text: The universe of The Infinite Black 2 is not a single infinite void, but a collection of distinct Solar Systems (Maps) connected by a network of Jump Gates.

docs.title: The Map Grid
docs.text: Every Solar System is a 15x15 Grid of Sectors. * Coordinates: Ranges from (0,0) in the bottom-left to (14,14) in the top-right. * Traversal: Crossing the edge of a map does not loop you around; you must use a Jump Gate to leave the system.

docs.title: Navigation
docs.text: To travel the universe, a Captain must move their fleet through individual Sectors. * Movement: Click an adjacent sector to engage engines. * Speed: Movement speed is determined by your slowest ship (or largest hull). * Safety: Some systems are patrolled by the Earth Defense Force (Safe), while others are lawless warzones (PvP). See Map Security Ratings for details.

docs.title: Topics
docs.text:
• Maps & Security Ratings – Understanding the systems.
  • Sectors & Collision – Stacking limits and how to avoid taking damage.
  • Jump Gates – How to warp between Solar Systems.

docs.title: Maps
docs.text: Placeholder text

docs.title: Security Rating
docs.text:
Security Rating (SR) defines how dangerous a sector of space is in The Infinite Black 2. It directly affects the strength of enemy ships, the risk of travel, and the level of preparation required to survive.

Security Ratings range from SR1 (safest) to SR9 (most dangerous).

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
As you move farther from protected space, security decreases and threats escalate. Higher Security Ratings feature:

  • Stronger and more aggressive enemy ships

  • Higher-level equipment and builds on NPCs

  • Increased likelihood of rapid ship destruction if unprepared

Progression Warning

Entering a higher Security Rating than your ship can handle will almost always result in destruction.

docs.title: Security Rating Scale
docs.text: Below is a breakdown of each Security Rating, including placeholder enemy level ranges. These ranges are indicative and may be adjusted as balance changes.

docs.title: SR1
docs.text:
• NPC Ship Levels: (Placeholder) 1–10

  • Maximum NPC Ships per Sector: (Placeholder) 3

  • Recommended Player Level: (Placeholder) 1–10

  • Recommended Player Ship Hull: (Placeholder) T1–T2

  • Special Notes: Starter-friendly space with minimal risk.

docs.title: SR2
docs.text:
• NPC Ship Levels: (Placeholder) 10–20

  • Maximum NPC Ships per Sector: (Placeholder) 4

  • Recommended Player Level: (Placeholder) 10–20

  • Recommended Player Ship Hull: (Placeholder) T2

  • Special Notes: Slightly increased enemy density and damage.

docs.title: SR3
docs.text:
• NPC Ship Levels: (Placeholder) 20–35

  • Maximum NPC Ships per Sector: (Placeholder) 5

  • Recommended Player Level: (Placeholder) 20–35

  • Recommended Player Ship Hull: (Placeholder) T2–T3

  • Special Notes: Enemies begin appearing in coordinated groups.

docs.title: SR4
docs.text:
• NPC Ship Levels: (Placeholder) 35–50

  • Maximum NPC Ships per Sector: (Placeholder) 6

  • Recommended Player Level: (Placeholder) 35–50

  • Recommended Player Ship Hull: (Placeholder) T3

  • Special Notes: Noticeable jump in survivability and damage output.

docs.title: SR5
docs.text:
• NPC Ship Levels: (Placeholder) 50–70

  • Maximum NPC Ships per Sector: (Placeholder) 7

  • Recommended Player Level: (Placeholder) 50–70

  • Recommended Player Ship Hull: (Placeholder) T3–T4

  • Special Notes: Mid-game difficulty spike; optimized builds recommended.

docs.title: SR6
docs.text:
• NPC Ship Levels: (Placeholder) 70–90

  • Maximum NPC Ships per Sector: (Placeholder) 8

  • Recommended Player Level: (Placeholder) 70–90

  • Recommended Player Ship Hull: (Placeholder) T4

  • Special Notes: Sustained combat capability required to survive.

docs.title: SR7
docs.text:
• NPC Ship Levels: (Placeholder) 90–110

  • Maximum NPC Ships per Sector: (Placeholder) 9

  • Recommended Player Level: (Placeholder) 90–110

  • Recommended Player Ship Hull: (Placeholder) T4–T5

  • Special Notes: Elite NPCs with strong synergies and burst damage.

docs.title: SR8
docs.text:
• NPC Ship Levels: (Placeholder) 110–130

  • Maximum NPC Ships per Sector: (Placeholder) 10

  • Recommended Player Level: (Placeholder) 110–130

  • Recommended Player Ship Hull: (Placeholder) T5

  • Special Notes: Extremely punishing; mistakes are rarely survivable.

docs.title: SR9
docs.text:
• NPC Ship Levels: (Placeholder) 130–150

  • Maximum NPC Ships per Sector: (Placeholder) 12

  • Recommended Player Level: (Placeholder) 130–150

  • Recommended Player Ship Hull: (Placeholder) T5 (Fully Optimized)

  • Special Notes: Highest danger level in the game. Full preparation required.

docs.title: Practical Effects of Security Rating
docs.text:
Security Rating influences more than just enemy strength:

  • Travel Risk: Higher SR sectors leave little time to react to ambushes

  • Ship Loss: Destruction is common in high SR without proper defenses

  • Preparation Requirements: Shield tier, weapon optimization, and build synergy become mandatory

docs.title: Tips for Pilots
docs.text:
• Progress gradually through Security Ratings rather than skipping ahead

  • Treat each SR increase as a gear and build check

  • If enemies feel overwhelming, retreat and improve your ship before advancing

Survival Rule

If you can’t comfortably win multiple fights in a sector, you’re not ready for its Security Rating.
